definition: The process of moving from the free WhatsApp Business App to the WhatsApp Business API when a business outgrows the App's limitations. This involves porting your existing phone number, setting up a WhatsApp Business Account (WABA) through Meta Business Manager, connecting through a BSP or the Cloud API directly, and accepting that chat history and some data won't carry over.
target_audience: Growing businesses that have hit the WhatsApp Business App's walls -- the 256-recipient broadcast cap, single-device limitation, lack of automation, or need for multi-agent support. Typically businesses with 500+ customer conversations per month or those needing CRM integration, chatbots, or broadcast campaigns at scale.
key_features: Number porting lets you keep your existing WhatsApp number so customers don't need to save a new contact. The migration process itself is relatively quick (minutes, not days). You gain access to all API features immediately after migration: multi-agent inboxes, chatbot automation, broadcast campaigns to unlimited recipients, CRM integrations, WhatsApp Flows, and CTWA ad support. Template messages carry over at the WABA level if migrating between BSPs. Meta's Cloud API is now the only supported hosting option (On-Premise API was discontinued in October 2025).
limitations: Chat history is permanently lost -- the App's message data does not migrate to the API. There is no way to transfer conversations, media, or insights from the App to the API. You must back up chat history manually from the App (Android or iOS export) before initiating migration. The App must be deleted from the phone before the number can be registered on the API. During migration, there is a brief period (typically under 10 minutes, often just seconds) where the number is in transition. You cannot run the App and API simultaneously on the same number. Once migrated to the API, you cannot easily go back to the App without losing API configuration.
pricing: The migration itself is free -- Meta does not charge for porting a number from the App to the API. However, moving to the API introduces new costs: Meta's per-message fees (marketing messages from ~$0.02 to ~$0.22 depending on region, utility and authentication are cheaper), plus BSP fees (ranging from $0 to $162+/month depending on provider, plus possible per-message markups). The WhatsApp Business App is free with no per-message charges, so migration represents a shift from zero cost to a variable cost structure. First 1,000 service conversations per month are free on the API.
scalability_ceiling: This item is about the migration process itself, so the scalability ceiling is really about what you're migrating to. The API supports Tier 0 (250/day) through Tier 4 (Unlimited) messaging, with automatic tier upgrades every 6 hours based on quality and volume. The App caps at 256 broadcast recipients and a single device -- that's usually the trigger for migration. Post-migration, the ceiling is effectively removed for most business use cases.
setup_complexity: Complexity: 3 out of 5. The process is straightforward but has several steps that need to be done in the right order. Steps for a WordPress site owner: (1) Back up all chat history from the WhatsApp Business App (Settings > Chats > Export Chat for each important conversation). (2) Create a Meta Business Account at business.facebook.com if you don't have one. (3) Complete Meta Business Verification (submit business documents -- takes 1-5 business days). (4) Choose and sign up with a BSP (Wati, 360dialog, Interakt, etc.) or set up the Cloud API directly. (5) In the BSP's onboarding flow, add your phone number -- this triggers a 6-digit OTP verification via SMS or voice call. (6) Delete the WhatsApp Business App from your phone (required before the number can register on the API). (7) Complete the verification and your number goes live on the API. (8) Create and submit message templates for approval. (9) Set up automations, integrations, and your team inbox. Total time: 1-3 hours for the technical migration, plus 1-5 days waiting for Meta Business Verification if not already completed.
migration_complexity: Number porting: You keep your existing phone number. The BSP initiates the migration, and you verify ownership via a 6-digit OTP sent to the number. Ensure 2FA is disabled on the source account before starting. Downtime: Minimal -- typically seconds to under 10 minutes. Messages sent during the brief transition are queued and delivered once migration completes. Some providers report zero downtime. Chat history: Completely lost. The App's message history, media, and business insights do not transfer to the API. This is the biggest pain point of migration. Back up everything before starting. Templates: If migrating from one BSP/WABA to another, approved templates carry over at the WABA level. But when first moving from App to API, you start fresh and need to create and submit templates for approval. Display name and profile: Your business display name, profile photo, and about section are re-configured during API setup. The display name must comply with Meta's naming guidelines and is reviewed during verification. Contacts: Customer contacts from the App do not automatically transfer. You need to import your contact list into your BSP or CRM separately. Rollback: Going back to the App after migrating to the API is technically possible but means losing all API configuration, templates, and integrations. It's effectively a one-way door for most businesses.
data_compliance: Migration itself does not create additional GDPR concerns beyond what the API introduces. The key compliance consideration is that during migration, you are moving from the App (which stores data on-device) to the API (which processes data through Meta's Cloud API servers, hosted in EU data centers for EU-configured accounts). You must ensure customer consent covers API-based messaging. If you collected opt-ins for App-based messaging, review whether those consents cover automated API messaging and marketing templates. Recommended: refresh opt-ins post-migration with a clear disclosure about the change in messaging infrastructure.
verification_path: Migration does not affect your verification status. If you had no verification on the App, you start unverified on the API and can apply for OBA (Official Business Account) blue tick once you meet Meta's notability criteria. If you were using Meta Verified on the App (paid subscription), that does not transfer to the API -- the API uses the free OBA path instead. Meta Business Verification (submitting business documents) is a separate requirement for the API and must be completed to unlock higher messaging tiers.
message_template_rules: After migrating to the API, all outbound messages outside the 24-hour customer service window require pre-approved templates. This is a significant operational change from the App, where you could send freeform messages anytime. Templates must be submitted in one of three categories (Marketing, Utility, Authentication) and approved by Meta before use. Plan your template library before migration so you're not stuck waiting for approvals on day one.
messaging_limits: New API accounts start at Tier 0 (250 unique recipients per day). This can feel like a downgrade from the App's unlimited 1-to-1 messaging, but tier upgrades happen automatically every 6 hours if you maintain quality and volume. Most active businesses reach Tier 2 (10,000/day) within 1-2 weeks. The frequency cap of ~2 marketing messages per user per day is enforced by Meta regardless of tier.
ai_automation_policy: Migration opens the door to chatbot automation that the App doesn't support. However, per Meta's January 2026 policy, only structured purpose-driven AI workflows are allowed -- no general-purpose AI chatbots. Plan your automation strategy as part of the migration, not as an afterthought.
quality_risk: New API accounts start with no quality history, so your quality rating builds from scratch. Early mistakes (poor targeting, irrelevant templates, high block rates) can tank your rating quickly, making it hard to scale. Best practice: start with utility messages (order confirmations, shipping updates) to build a positive quality track record before ramping up marketing campaigns.
ads_integration: After migration, you can fully leverage Click-to-WhatsApp Ads (CTWA) with the 72-hour free messaging window and CAPI conversion tracking. The App supports basic CTWA but without the automation, tracking, or scale that the API enables. This is often a key driver for migration -- businesses running CTWA campaigns need the API's inbox and automation to handle the volume of incoming conversations.
ecommerce_features: Migration to the API unlocks product catalog integration in WhatsApp, WooCommerce/Shopify sync through BSPs, and in-chat ordering capabilities. For WordPress/WooCommerce store owners, this is often the tipping point -- the App's manual catalog management doesn't scale, while the API can auto-sync your product database and send automated order updates.
wpchat_relevance: WPChat.com plays a valuable role during and after migration. Before migration, WPChat can serve as the WhatsApp entry point on your WordPress site, connecting visitors to your Business App number. After migration to the API, WPChat continues to work as the front-end widget -- visitors click the chat button, and the conversation flows into your BSP-managed API inbox instead of the App. No changes needed on the WPChat side when you migrate. WPChat's AI-powered FAQs can also reduce the load on your new API setup by handling common questions before they hit your team inbox.
best_for: Businesses hitting the App's 256-recipient broadcast limit. Teams needing multi-agent support (more than 1-4 devices). Companies running Click-to-WhatsApp ad campaigns at scale. E-commerce stores that need automated order notifications and catalog sync. Any business that needs CRM integration, chatbot automation, or detailed analytics.
not_suited_for: Businesses with fewer than a few hundred customer interactions per month -- the App is still sufficient and free. Solo operators who handle all conversations personally and don't need automation. Businesses not ready to invest in a BSP subscription or the learning curve of template-based messaging.
cta_angle: Whether you are still on the WhatsApp Business App or have already made the jump to the API, WPChat.com makes sure your WordPress site has a professional, always-visible chat entry point. It works with both the App and the API, so you can install it now and it will carry over seamlessly when you migrate.
